fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\colon B\to A,\epsilon \colon f\circ g =1_{B}\right \rparen \mathclose {} be a section of a function f\colon A\to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} P &:\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ P &\equiv \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen p : g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} = x\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} \cong _{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {}\left \lparen g \mathopen {}\left \lparen f x\right \rparen \mathclose {} ,\epsilon \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} =_{\mathsf {fiber}\,f\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen fx\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {refl}\right \rparen \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.
If A is a proposition, then for all x,y:A the identification type x=y is contractible.
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via .

If A is contractible and B\colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is a family of contractible types, then the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is contractible.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s\colon B \to A be a section of r\colon A\to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use and .

For any type A we have a proposition \mathsf {toProp}\,{A} equipped with a unit map \eta _{\mathsf {prop}}\colon A \to \mathsf {toProp}\,{A}; moreover, we have the following induction principle: for any family of propositions B\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A \to \mathsf {Prop}, any function b\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} B \mathopen {}\left \lparen \eta _{\mathsf {prop}}\,x\right \rparen \mathclose {} extends to a function b^\sharp \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x\colon \mathsf {toProp}\,A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} Bx.
When we have an element of \mathsf {toProp}\,A, we will say that A is inhabited.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products () via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from and .
It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

fr:ol.fr:li: When n=-2, we recall from that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from that being a proposition is a proposition.

For n \geq 0, we assume by induction that being n-1-truncated is a proposition to prove that being n-truncated is a proposition. In other words, we must show that \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}\mathsf {isTruncated}\, \mathopen {}\left \lparen n-1\right \rparen \mathclose {} \,A is a proposition. By our inductive hypothesis, this is a dependent product of propositions ().

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a dependent function f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx}, if each component Bx is contractible with centre fx then the dependent product \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx} is contractible with centre f.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} be a family of propositions; fixing f : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{Bx}, it suffices to show any other g :\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx may be identified with f. As B is a family of propositions, we know that each Bx is contractible with centre fx. Therefore, the closure of contractible types under dependent products () shows that \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x:A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx is contractible with centre f.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.

A reflexive graph \mathcal {A} is called univalent when for all x: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y: \mathopen {}\left \lvert \mathcal {A}\right \rvert \mathclose {} \right \rparen \mathclose {} }} x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y is a proposition, or (equivalently), is contractible with centre \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {A}}{x}\right \rparen \mathclose {} . We shall refer to a univalent reflexive graph as a path object.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

Let A be a contractible type with centre a. For any family of types B\colon A \to \mathsf {Type}, we may define a function \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\colon B a \to \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x : A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x : A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x : A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x : A\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}Bx satisfying \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\,b\,a = b.

Let \chi _x \colon a = x be the witness of contraction for any x:A. Given b : Ba and x:A, we define \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\,b\,x : Bx by transporting b along the identification \chi _a^{-1} \cdot \chi _x \colon a = x:
Then we compute \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\,b\,a \equiv \mathopen {}\left \lparen \chi _a^{-1}\cdot \chi _a\right \rparen \mathclose {} _* b = \mathsf {refl}_*b = b.

For any precategory C, the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen D : \mathsf {Precategory}\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{D\cong C} of isomorphisms of precategories onto C is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type}\\ D_1&:D_0\to D_0\to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_0&: D_0\to C_0\\ F_1&: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} D_1xy\to C \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_0x,F_0y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ h_1 &: \mathsf {isBiinvertible}\,F_0\\ h_2 &: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathsf {isPropMap} \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_1xy\right \rparen \mathclose {} \\ h_3 &: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} \mathsf {isSurjection} \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_1xy\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_0&: \mathsf {Type}\\ D_1&:D_0\to D_0\to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_0&: D_0\cong C_0\\ F_1&: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y: D_0\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} D_1xy\cong C \mathopen {}\left \lparen F_0x,F_0y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

\begin {aligned} D_1&: \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_1&: \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }} D_1xy\cong C \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\right \rparen \mathclose {} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (, ):
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} D_1&: \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {}\left \lvert C\right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {Set}\\ F_1&: D_1 = \lambda {x,y}\mathpunct {.}{C \mathopen {}\left \lparen x,y\right \rparen \mathclose {} } \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.

A precategory C is called univalent when for any x\in C, the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {}\left \lparen y\in C\right \rparen \mathclose {} }}y\cong x of isomorphisms onto x is contractible.
